```json
}
```

```bash
# Create the policy
aws iam create-policy \
  --policy-name ClusterAutoscalerPolicy \
  --policy-document file://cluster-autoscaler-policy.json \
  --description "Policy for EKS Cluster Autoscaler"
```

---

## Step 2: Configure Managed Node Groups

### Option A: eksctl (Recommended)

```yaml
# cluster-config.yaml
apiVersion: eksctl.io/v1alpha5
kind: ClusterConfig

metadata:
  name: my-cluster
  region: us-east-1
  version: "1.28"

iam:
  withOIDC: true  # Required for IRSA

managedNodeGroups:
  # General purpose node group
  - name: general-ng
    instanceType: m5.xlarge
    minSize: 2
    maxSize: 10
    desiredCapacity: 3
    availabilityZones: ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b", "us-east-1c"]
    iam:
      withAddonPolicies:
        autoScaler: true          # Attaches autoscaler IAM policy automatically
    labels:
      role: general
      node-type: on-demand
    tags:
      # Required tags for autoscaler discovery
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled: "true"
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster: "owned"
      # Optional: cost allocation
      Environment: production
      Team: platform
    updateConfig:
      maxUnavailable: 1           # Rolling update safety

  # Compute-optimized node group
  - name: compute-ng
    instanceType: c5.2xlarge
    minSize: 0                    # Scale to zero when not needed
    maxSize: 20
    desiredCapacity: 0
    iam:
      withAddonPolicies:
        autoScaler: true
    labels:
      role: compute
      node-type: on-demand
    taints:
      - key: workload-type
        value: compute-intensive
        effect: NoSchedule        # Only schedule matching pods here
    tags:
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled: "true"
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster: "owned"
      # Autoscaler uses these for resource hints when scaling from zero
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/node-template/resources/cpu: "8"
      k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/node-template/resources/memory: "16Gi"
```

```bash
# Deploy cluster with node groups
eksctl create cluster -f cluster-config.yaml
```

### Option B: Terraform

```hcl
# main.tf
module "eks" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/eks/aws"
  version = "~> 20.0"

  cluster_name    = "my-cluster"
  cluster_version = "1.28"

  vpc_id     = module.vpc.vpc_id
  subnet_ids = module.vpc.private_subnets

  # Required for IRSA
  enable_irsa = true

  eks_managed_node_groups = {
    general = {
      name           = "general-ng"
      instance_types = ["m5.xlarge"]

      min_size     = 2
      max_size     = 10
      desired_size = 3

      labels = {
        role      = "general"
        node-type = "on-demand"
      }

      tags = {
        "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled"    = "true"
        "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster" = "owned"
      }
    }

    spot = {
      name           = "spot-ng"
      instance_types = ["m5.large", "m5a.large", "m4.large"]  # Multiple types for spot availability
      capacity_type  = "SPOT"

      min_size     = 0
      max_size     = 30
      desired_size = 0

      labels = {
        role          = "spot"
        "node-type"   = "spot"
      }

      taints = [{
        key    = "spot-instance"
        value  = "true"
        effect = "NO_SCHEDULE"
      }]

      tags = {
        "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled"    = "true"
        "k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster" = "owned"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Step 3: Configure IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)

```bash
# Step 3b: Create IAM service account
```

## Step 4: Deploy Cluster Autoscaler

```yaml
# cluster-autoscaler.yaml
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler
  namespace: kube-system
  annotations:
    # Set by eksctl iamserviceaccount — verify this ARN
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/eksctl-my-cluster-addon-iamserviceaccount-Role

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["events", "endpoints"]
    verbs: ["create", "patch"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "nodes", "namespaces", "persistentvolumeclaims"]
    verbs: ["watch", "list", "get"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]
    verbs: ["update"]
  - apiGroups: ["extensions", "apps"]
    resources: ["replicasets", "statefulsets", "daemonsets"]
    verbs: ["watch", "list", "get"]
  - apiGroups: ["policy"]
    resources: ["poddisruptionbudgets"]
    verbs: ["watch", "list"]
  - apiGroups: ["storage.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["storageclasses", "csinodes", "csidrivers", "csistoragecapacities"]
    verbs: ["watch", "list", "get"]
  - apiGroups: ["batch"]
    resources: ["jobs"]
    verbs: ["watch", "list", "get"]
  - apiGroups: ["coordination.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["leases"]
    verbs: ["create"]
  - apiGroups: ["coordination.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["leases"]
    resourceNames: ["cluster-autoscaler"]
    verbs: ["get", "update"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: cluster-autoscaler
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: cluster-autoscaler
    namespace: kube-system

---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    app: cluster-autoscaler
  annotations:
    # Prevent autoscaler from evicting itself
    cluster-autoscaler.kubernetes.io/safe-to-evict: "false"
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cluster-autoscaler
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cluster-autoscaler
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "8085"
    spec:
      priorityClassName: system-cluster-critical
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 65534
        fsGroup: 65534
      serviceAccountName: cluster-autoscaler
      
      # Run on control plane nodes if possible
      tolerations:
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
          effect: NoSchedule
      
      containers:
        - name: cluster-autoscaler
          image: registry.k8s.io/autoscaling/cluster-autoscaler:v1.28.2
          
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 600Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 600Mi
          
          command:
            - ./cluster-autoscaler
            # Logging
            - --v=4
            - --stderrthreshold=info
            # Cloud provider
            - --cloud-provider=aws
            - --region=us-east-1
            # Node group discovery via tags
            - --node-group-auto-discovery=asg:tag=k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/enabled,k8s.io/cluster-autoscaler/my-cluster
            # Scaling behavior
            - --expander=least-waste          # Choose node group that wastes least resources
            - --balance-similar-node-groups   # Balance nodes across similar groups
            - --skip-nodes-with-local-storage=false
            - --skip-nodes-with-system-pods=false
            # Scale-down configuration
            - --scale-down-enabled=true
            - --scale-down-delay-after-add=10m      # Wait after scale-up before scale-down
            - --scale-down-unneeded-time=10m        # Node must be unneeded this long
            - --scale-down-utilization-threshold=0.5 # Scale down if utilization < 50%
            # Scan interval
            - --scan-interval=10s
          
          env:
            - name: AWS_REGION
              value: us-east-1
          
          volumeMounts:
            - name: ssl-certs
              mountPath: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
              readOnly: true
          
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health-check
              port: 8085
            initialDelaySeconds: 20
            periodSeconds: 10
      
      volumes:
        - name: ssl-certs
          hostPath:
            path: /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
```

```bash
kubectl apply -f cluster-autoscaler.yaml

# Verify deployment
kubectl get deployment cluster-autoscaler -n kube-system
kubectl logs -f deployment/cluster-autoscaler -n kube-system | grep -E "scale|node"
```

---

## Step 5: Expander Strategies

```bash
# Choose the right expander for your use case:

# least-waste (default recommendation)
# → Minimizes wasted CPU/memory after scaling
- --expander=least-waste

# random
# → Randomly selects node group (good for spot diversity)
- --expander=random

# most-pods
# → Selects group that schedules the most pods
- --expander=most-pods

# priority (advanced: use a ConfigMap to rank node groups)
- --expander=priority
```

```yaml
# Priority expander ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: cluster-autoscaler-priority-expander
  namespace: kube-system
data:
  priorities: |-
    10:
      - .*spot-ng.*        # Prefer spot instances (lower cost)
    20:
      - .*general-ng.*     # Fall back to on-demand
    30:
      - .*compute-ng.*     # Last resort: specialized nodes
```

# Configuring CloudWatch Container Insights for ECS Task Memory Monitoring

Based on the available context, I want to be transparent about what is and isn't directly documented before providing guidance.

---

## Important Transparency Note

The source documentation **only briefly confirms** that CloudWatch integrates with ECS covering "Logs, metrics, Container Insights." The detailed configuration steps below are derived from **general AWS knowledge**, not from explicit instructions in the provided documentation. Please verify steps against the official AWS ECS Developer Guide and CloudWatch Container Insights documentation.

---

## What the Documentation Does Confirm

- CloudWatch is an **integrated service** with ECS/Fargate
- The integration covers **logs, metrics, and Container Insights**
- This applies across ECS launch types (EC2, Fargate, and potentially External/ECS Anywhere, though Container Insights support may be limited for the External launch type)

---

## Configuration Overview

### Step 1: Enable Container Insights at the Cluster Level

Container Insights must be enabled on the **ECS Cluster** itself:

```bash
# On an existing cluster
aws ecs update-cluster-settings \
  --cluster my-cluster \
  --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled

# At cluster creation
aws ecs create-cluster \
  --cluster-name my-cluster \
  --settings name=containerInsights,value=enabled
```

---

### Step 2: Ensure the Task Execution Role Has Required Permissions

The Task Execution Role needs permissions to write logs and metrics to CloudWatch:

```json
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": [
    "logs:CreateLogGroup",
    "logs:CreateLogStream",
    "logs:PutLogEvents",
    "cloudwatch:PutMetricData"
  ],
  "Resource": "*"
}
```

---

### Step 3: Configure the Task Definition

Set memory limits and the `awslogs` log driver in your container definitions:

```json
{
  "family": "my-task",
  "executionRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/ecsTaskExecutionRole",
  "memory": "2048",
  "containerDefinitions": [
    {
      "name": "my-container",
      "image": "nginx:latest",
      "memory": 2048,
      "memoryReservation": 1024,
      "logConfiguration": {
        "logDriver": "awslogs",
        "options": {
          "awslogs-group": "/ecs/my-task",
          "awslogs-region": "us-east-1",
          "awslogs-stream-prefix": "ecs"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

> **Note:** `memory` is the hard limit; `memoryReservation` is the soft limit. Container Insights tracks both.

---

### Step 4: Create the CloudWatch Log Group

```bash
aws logs create-log-group --log-group-name /ecs/my-task

# Optional: set retention policy
aws logs put-retention-policy \
  --log-group-name /ecs/my-task \
  --retention-in-days 30
```

---

### Step 5: Set Memory Alarms Using Container Insights Metrics

Container Insights publishes to the `ECS/ContainerInsights` namespace. Key memory metrics:

| Metric | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `MemoryUtilized` | Actual memory used (MB) |
| `MemoryReserved` | Memory reserved by task definition (MB) |
| `MemoryUtilization` | Percentage of reserved memory in use |

```bash
aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name "ECS-High-Memory-Utilization" \
  --alarm-description "Triggers when ECS task memory exceeds 80%" \
  --namespace "ECS/ContainerInsights" \
  --metric-name "MemoryUtilization" \
  --dimensions \
      Name=ClusterName,Value=my-cluster \
      Name=ServiceName,Value=my-service \
  --statistic Average \
  --period 300 \
  --threshold 80 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold \
  --evaluation-periods 2 \
  --alarm-actions arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:ACCOUNT:my-alert-topic \
  --treat-missing-data notBreaching
```

---

### Step 6: Optionally Connect Alarms to Auto Scaling

```bash
# Register scalable target
aws application-autoscaling register-scalable-target \
  --service-namespace ecs \
  --scalable-dimension ecs:service:DesiredCount \
  --resource-id service/my-cluster/my-service \
  --min-capacity 1 \
  --max-capacity 10
```

# Building an Event-Driven Pipeline with S3 and Lambda

## Architecture Overview

The pipeline follows a straightforward event-driven pattern: a file upload to S3 automatically triggers a Lambda function asynchronously, which then processes the uploaded file. No servers need to be provisioned — Lambda manages the underlying compute automatically.

```
S3 Bucket (file upload)
       ↓
S3 Object Event (s3:ObjectCreated)
       ↓
Lambda Function (async invocation)
       ↓
File Processing Logic
```

---

## Core Components

### 1. S3 as the Event Source
- S3 acts as an **asynchronous invocation source** for Lambda
- When a file is uploaded, S3 emits an **object event** and fires it to Lambda **without waiting for a response**
- Events can be filtered by:
  - **Prefix** (e.g., only files in `uploads/`)
  - **Suffix** (e.g., only `.csv` files)
  - **Event type** (created, deleted, modified)

### 2. Lambda as the Processor
- Receives a structured **event payload** from S3 containing:
  - Bucket name
  - Object key (file path)
  - Event type
  - Object size and metadata
- Executes your processing logic against the uploaded file

---

## Step-by-Step Implementation

### Step 1: Configure S3 Event Notifications

Point your S3 bucket's notification settings at your Lambda function ARN:

```json
{
  "LambdaFunctionConfigurations": [
    {
      "LambdaFunctionArn": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:function:ProcessUpload",
      "Events": ["s3:ObjectCreated:*"],
      "Filter": {
        "Key": {
          "FilterRules": [
            { "Name": "prefix", "Value": "uploads/" },
            { "Name": "suffix", "Value": ".csv" }
          ]
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

> ⚠️ **Important**: Use prefix/suffix filters to prevent processed output files from re-triggering the function in an infinite loop.

---

### Step 2: Write the Lambda Handler

```python
import boto3

def handler(event, context):
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    
    # S3 can batch multiple records in one event
    for record in event['Records']:
        bucket = record['s3']['bucket']['name']
        key    = record['s3']['object']['key']
        size   = record['s3']['object']['size']
        
        print(f"Processing: s3://{bucket}/{key} ({size} bytes)")
        
        # Retrieve and process the file
        obj  = s3.get_object(Bucket=bucket, Key=key)
        data = obj['Body'].read()
        
        process_file(data)  # Your custom processing logic

def process_file(data):
    # Parse CSV, transform data, write to database, etc.
    pass
```

---

### Step 3: Configure IAM Permissions

Two permission grants are required:

| Direction | Permission Needed |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Lambda → S3** | `s3:GetObject` on the source bucket (via Lambda's execution role) |
| **S3 → Lambda** | `lambda:InvokeFunction` (resource-based policy on the Lambda function) |

```json
// Lambda resource-based policy (allows S3 to invoke it)
{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Principal": { "Service": "s3.amazonaws.com" },
  "Action": "lambda:InvokeFunction",
  "Resource": "arn:aws:lambda:us-east-1:123456789:function:ProcessUpload",
  "Condition": {
    "ArnLike": {
      "AWS:SourceArn": "arn:aws:s3:::your-bucket-name"
    }
  }
}
```

---

## Key Operational Considerations

### Reliability
- **Async retries**: Because S3→Lambda is asynchronous, Lambda will automatically retry on failure
- **Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)**: Configure a DLQ (SQS or SNS) to capture events that exhaust all retries
- **Idempotency**: S3 may deliver duplicate events — design your handler to produce the same result if run multiple times on the same file

### Scalability
- Lambda has **concurrency limits** — large bursts of simultaneous uploads may cause throttling
- Consider setting **reserved concurrency** to protect downstream systems from being overwhelmed

---

## Extending the Pipeline

Once Lambda processes the file, you can chain additional services:

```
S3 Upload → Lambda (process file)
                  ↓
           EventBridge (route results)
                  ↓
    ┌─────────────┴─────────────┐
    ↓                           ↓
Kinesis Stream             Amazon MQ
(stream processing)    (message fan-out)
    ↓
Lambda (polling consumer)
```

| Extension | Use Case |
|-----------|----------|
| **EventBridge** | Route processed results or failures to other services |
| **Kinesis Streams** | Handle high-throughput record streaming with downstream Lambda consumers via stream-based polling |
| **Amazon MQ** | Fan out processing results to multiple consumers via a message broker |

---

## Summary Checklist

- [ ] Create S3 bucket and configure event notifications with appropriate filters
- [ ] Write Lambda handler that parses `event['Records']` for bucket/key details
- [ ] Attach IAM execution role to Lambda with `s3:GetObject` permission
- [ ] Grant S3 permission to invoke Lambda via resource-based policy
- [ ] Configure a Dead Letter Queue for failed async invocations
- [ ] Test with a sample file upload and verify CloudWatch logs
- [ ] Design handler to be **idempotent** against duplicate event delivery

# Configuring ALB for HTTPS Termination and HTTP-to-HTTPS Redirect

## ⚠️ Important Transparency Note

The available knowledge base **does not contain specific documentation** on this topic. The guidance below is based on **standard AWS ALB configuration knowledge**, not retrieved context. Please verify against the [official AWS Elastic Load Balancing documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/).

---

## Architecture Overview

```
Client
  │
  ├── HTTP :80  ──► ALB Port 80 Listener ──► 301 Redirect to HTTPS
  │
  └── HTTPS :443 ──► ALB Port 443 Listener
                          │
                          │ [TLS Terminated at ALB]
                          │ [Plain HTTP forwarded internally]
                          ▼
                     Target Group
                  (ECS Tasks / Lambda / EC2)
```

---

## Step 1: Prerequisites

### ACM Certificate
You must have an SSL/TLS certificate provisioned in **AWS Certificate Manager (ACM)** in the **same region** as your ALB.

```yaml
# Request a public certificate (DNS validation recommended)
ACMCertificate:
  Type: AWS::CertificateManager::Certificate
  Properties:
    DomainName: example.com
    SubjectAlternativeNames:
      - "*.example.com"
    ValidationMethod: DNS
```

---

## Step 2: Security Group Configuration

Allow inbound traffic on both ports 80 and 443:

```yaml
ALBSecurityGroup:
  Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
  Properties:
    GroupDescription: ALB Security Group
    VpcId: !Ref VpcId
    SecurityGroupIngress:
      # Allow HTTP (needed for redirect to work)
      - IpProtocol: tcp
        FromPort: 80
        ToPort: 80
        CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0
      - IpProtocol: tcp
        FromPort: 80
        ToPort: 80
        CidrIpv6: "::/0"
      # Allow HTTPS
      - IpProtocol: tcp
        FromPort: 443
        ToPort: 443
        CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0
      - IpProtocol: tcp
        FromPort: 443
        ToPort: 443
        CidrIpv6: "::/0"
    SecurityGroupEgress:
      # Forward decrypted traffic to targets
      - IpProtocol: tcp
        FromPort: 80
        ToPort: 80
        DestinationSecurityGroupId: !Ref TargetSecurityGroup
```

---

## Step 3: ALB Resource

```yaml
ApplicationLoadBalancer:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::LoadBalancer
  Properties:
    Name: my-application-alb
    Scheme: internet-facing          # or internal
    Type: application
    IpAddressType: dualstack         # IPv4 + IPv6
    SecurityGroups:
      - !Ref ALBSecurityGroup
    Subnets:                         # Must span >= 2 AZs
      - !Ref PublicSubnet1
      - !Ref PublicSubnet2
    LoadBalancerAttributes:
      - Key: idle_timeout.timeout_seconds
        Value: "60"
      - Key: routing.http2.enabled
        Value: "true"
      - Key: access_logs.s3.enabled
        Value: "true"
      - Key: access_logs.s3.bucket
        Value: !Ref LogsBucket
```

---

## Step 4: HTTPS Listener — TLS Termination (Port 443)

```yaml
HTTPSListener:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::Listener
  Properties:
    LoadBalancerArn: !Ref ApplicationLoadBalancer
    Port: 443
    Protocol: HTTPS
    # Prefer TLS 1.3 policy for modern security
    SslPolicy: ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06
    Certificates:
      - CertificateArn: !Ref ACMCertificate
    DefaultActions:
      - Type: forward
        TargetGroupArn: !Ref TargetGroup
```

### SSL Policy Selection Guide

| Policy | TLS Versions | Use Case |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-2-2021-06` | TLS 1.2 + 1.3 | ✅ Recommended default |
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-TLS13-1-3-2021-06` | TLS 1.3 only | Maximum security, may break older clients |
| `ELBSecurityPolicy-2016-08` | TLS 1.0–1.2 | Legacy — avoid if possible |

---

## Step 5: HTTP Listener — Redirect to HTTPS (Port 80)

```yaml
HTTPListener:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::Listener
  Properties:
    LoadBalancerArn: !Ref ApplicationLoadBalancer
    Port: 80
    Protocol: HTTP
    DefaultActions:
      - Type: redirect
        RedirectConfig:
          Protocol: HTTPS
          Port: "443"
          StatusCode: HTTP_301      # Permanent redirect (cacheable)
          # Preserve original request details
          Host: "#{host}"
          Path: "/#{path}"
          Query: "#{query}"
```

> **HTTP_301 vs HTTP_302:**
> - `HTTP_301` — Permanent redirect; browsers cache it. Use for production.
> - `HTTP_302` — Temporary redirect; not cached. Use during testing/migration.

---

## Step 6: Target Group

```yaml
TargetGroup:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::TargetGroup
  Properties:
    Name: my-target-group
    VpcId: !Ref VpcId
    Protocol: HTTP          # ALB forwards plain HTTP after TLS termination
    Port: 80
    TargetType: ip          # Use 'ip' for ECS Fargate, 'instance' for EC2
    HealthCheckProtocol: HTTP
    HealthCheckPath: /health
    HealthCheckIntervalSeconds: 30
    HealthyThresholdCount: 2
    UnhealthyThresholdCount: 3
    TargetGroupAttributes:
      - Key: deregistration_delay.timeout_seconds
        Value: "30"
      - Key: stickiness.enabled
        Value: "false"
```

---

## Step 7: Advanced Listener Rules (Optional)

Add path-based or host-based routing rules on the HTTPS listener:

```yaml
APIListenerRule:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::ListenerRule
  Properties:
    ListenerArn: !Ref HTTPSListener
    Priority: 10
    Conditions:
      - Field: path-pattern
        Values:
          - "/api/*"
    Actions:
      - Type: forward
        TargetGroupArn: !Ref APITargetGroup

# Fixed response for unmatched paths
DefaultRule:
  Type: AWS::ElasticLoadBalancingV2::ListenerRule
  Properties:
    ListenerArn: !Ref HTTPSListener
    Priority: 100
    Conditions:
      - Field: path-pattern
        Values:
          - "/*"
    Actions:
      - Type: fixed-response
        FixedResponseConfig:
          StatusCode: "404"
          ContentType: application/json
          MessageBody: '{"error": "Not found"}'
```

---

## Complete Traffic Flow Summary

```
1. Client → HTTP :80
   ALB Port 80 Listener
   └─► Action: redirect
       └─► 301 Location: https://example.com/original/path?query
           (Host, Path, Query preserved)

2. Client → HTTPS :443
   ALB Port 443 Listener
   └─► TLS handshake (ACM certificate presented)
   └─► Traffic decrypted at ALB
   └─► Listener rules evaluated (priority order)
   └─► Matched rule → forward to Target Group
   └─► Target Group → plain HTTP to target (ECS/EC2/Lambda)
```

---

## Key Considerations

| Concern | Recommendation |
|---------|---------------|
| **Certificate renewal** | Use ACM — auto-renews, no manual intervention |
| **Multiple domains** | Add additional certificates via `AddListenerCertificates` |
| **End-to-end encryption** | Use HTTPS on target group too (re-encryption mode) |
| **HSTS** | Configure at application layer, not ALB |
| **IPv6** | Use `dualstack` IP address type on ALB |
| **Logging** | Enable S3 access logs for audit/debugging |

---

> **Reminder:** This guidance is based on general AWS knowledge. The available knowledge base did not contain documentation on this specific topic. Always validate against the [official AWS ALB documentation](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/create-https-listener.html).

# Reliable Asynchronous Lambda Processing: Destinations, DLQs, and CloudWatch Alarms

## Important Transparency Note

The retrieved context explicitly states it **lacks sufficient information** to answer this question directly. The knowledge graph only confirms:
- Basic Lambda event source categories (async invocation exists as a concept)
- SQS integration via `AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole`
- CloudWatch Log Group for log storage

What follows draws heavily on established AWS architectural knowledge to fill those gaps. I'll clearly distinguish graph-confirmed facts from supplementary knowledge.

---

## The Core Problem Being Solved

Asynchronous Lambda invocations (from SNS, EventBridge, S3, etc.) share a fundamental characteristic confirmed by the graph: **"the caller does not wait for a response and events are processed asynchronously."** This creates a reliability challenge — if the caller has already moved on, who catches failures? The three mechanisms answer this question at different layers.

---

## Layer 1: Lambda Function Destinations

### What They Are
Destinations are routing rules configured directly on the Lambda function that determine where the **execution result** is sent after an async invocation completes — regardless of success or failure.

### How They Work
```
Async Invocation Completes
         │
    ┌────┴────┐
 SUCCESS   FAILURE (after retries exhausted)
    │           │
    ▼           ▼
On-Success  On-Failure
Destination Destination
(SQS/SNS/   (SQS/SNS/
EventBridge/ EventBridge/
Lambda)      Lambda)
```

### What the Destination Payload Contains
This is the key advantage over DLQs — destinations carry **rich metadata**:

| Field | Content |
|-------|---------|
| `requestContext` | Function ARN, request ID, condition (Success/Failure) |
| `requestPayload` | The original triggering event |
| `responseContext` | Status code, error details, function version |
| `responsePayload` | Function return value or error object |

### Retry Behavior Before Destination Triggers
Lambda automatically retries failed async invocations **twice** before routing to the On-Failure Destination. The maximum event age and retry attempts are configurable.

---

## Layer 2: SQS Dead-Letter Queues

### Graph-Confirmed Foundation
The graph confirms SQS integration with Lambda via `AWSLambdaSQSQueueExecutionRole`, which grants Lambda permission to **"receive and delete messages from SQS queues."** This role underpins the DLQ integration.

### DLQ vs. On-Failure Destination

| Characteristic | DLQ | On-Failure Destination |
|---------------|-----|----------------------|
| Payload | Original event only | Full event + execution metadata |
| Configuration | On the Lambda function | On the Lambda function |
| Supported targets | SQS, SNS | SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Lambda |
| Context richness | Low | High |
| Recommended for | Legacy/fallback | New architectures |

### Why Both Can Coexist
Configuring both simultaneously provides **defense-in-depth**:
- On-Failure Destination handles the primary failure routing with rich context
- DLQ acts as a secondary safety net if the destination itself fails to receive the event

### SQS-Specific Reliability Properties
- **Durability**: Messages persist until explicitly deleted (up to 14 days)
- **Visibility timeout**: Prevents duplicate processing during redrive operations
- **Dead-letter queue depth**: Monitorable metric that feeds directly into CloudWatch Alarms

---

## Layer 3: CloudWatch Alarms

### Graph-Confirmed Foundation
The graph confirms `aws.cloudwatch.log_group` as the destination for Lambda execution logs. CloudWatch Alarms operate on the **metrics** layer (distinct from logs) but form a unified observability system.

### Critical Lambda Metrics to Alarm On

| Metric | What It Indicates | Alarm Action |
|--------|------------------|--------------|
| `Errors` | Invocation failures occurring | Alert ops team |
| `DeadLetterErrors` | Lambda **cannot write to DLQ** — critical | Immediate escalation |
| `AsyncEventsDropped` | Events lost entirely (no DLQ configured) | Critical alert |
| `ConcurrentExecutions` | Approaching Lambda concurrency limits | Scale or throttle upstream |
| `Duration` | Approaching timeout threshold | Review function logic |

> **`DeadLetterErrors` deserves special attention**: This metric fires when the safety net itself is broken — Lambda tried to write to the DLQ but failed (wrong permissions, queue deleted, etc.). This is a silent failure mode that CloudWatch Alarms uniquely catch.

### The Observability Loop
```
Lambda Failure
      │
      ▼
Metric emitted to CloudWatch
      │
      ▼
Alarm threshold breached
      │
      ▼
SNS notification → Ops team / PagerDuty
      │
      ▼
Ops investigates DLQ / Destination queue
      │
      ▼
Fix root cause → Redrive messages from DLQ
```

---

## Complete Integrated Architecture

```
Async Event Source
(SNS / EventBridge / S3)
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Lambda Function             │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │ IAM Execution Role          │   │
│  │ Timeout: 1–900s             │   │
│  │ Retry: up to 2x on failure  │   │
│  └─────────────────────────────┘   │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
         │              │
      SUCCESS        FAILURE
         │              │
         ▼              ▼
  On-Success      On-Failure          DLQ
  Destination     Destination    (SQS fallback)
  (next step)     (rich payload) (original event)
                       │
                       ▼
              CloudWatch Logs
              CloudWatch Metrics
                       │
                       ▼
              CloudWatch Alarms
              ├── Errors
              ├── DeadLetterErrors  ← most critical
              ├── AsyncEventsDropped
              └── ConcurrentExecutions
                       │
                       ▼
              SNS Alert → Ops Response
```

---

## Reliability Guarantees by Layer

| Layer | Guarantee Provided |
|-------|-------------------|
| Async retries | At-least-once delivery attempt (up to 3 total) |
| On-Failure Destination | Failure context preserved for reprocessing |
| SQS DLQ | Durable event storage — no data loss on failure |
| CloudWatch Alarms | Proactive detection of failures and broken safety nets |
| Combined | End-to-end auditability from invocation to resolution |

---

## Summary

The three mechanisms operate at **complementary layers**:

1. **Destinations** handle the *routing* of outcomes — they tell Lambda where results go and carry enough context to enable intelligent downstream processing or automated remediation
2. **DLQs** handle *durability* — they ensure failed events are never silently dropped, providing a replayable store for recovery
3. **CloudWatch Alarms** handle *visibility* — they surface failures (including failures of the failure-handling mechanisms themselves) to human operators before silent accumulation becomes a crisis

Together they form a complete reliability loop: route failures → store them durably → alert humans → redrive after fixing the root cause.

# EKS OIDC + IRSA + Kubernetes Service Accounts: Complete Architecture

## Transparency Note

The provided knowledge graph and vector index **do not contain nodes or documents covering EKS, OIDC providers, IRSA, or Kubernetes service accounts**. The context explicitly states this limitation. The explanation below is based on well-established AWS documentation and architecture knowledge, not the retrieved context. I will clearly distinguish what comes from the graph versus general knowledge.

---

## The Core Problem Being Solved

Pods running in EKS need AWS permissions (e.g., read from S3, write to DynamoDB). The naive solution — embedding IAM access keys in the pod — creates serious security risks: leaked credentials, no rotation, no auditability. IRSA solves this by giving pods **temporary, automatically-rotated credentials** scoped to their specific identity.

---

## Component Map

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        EKS Cluster                              │
│                                                                 │
│   ┌─────────────┐     references    ┌──────────────────────┐   │
│   │     Pod     │ ─────────────────▶│  Kubernetes Service  │   │
│   │             │                   │     Account (KSA)    │   │
│   └─────────────┘                   └──────────┬───────────┘   │
│                                                │               │
│                                    annotation: │               │
│                                    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┼───────────────┘
                                                 │
                    ┌────────────────────────────▼───────────────┐
                    │              AWS IAM                        │
                    │                                             │
                    │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐  │
                    │   │           IAM Role                  │  │
                    │   │  Trust Policy: federated OIDC trust │  │
                    │   │  Permission Policy: S3, DynamoDB... │  │
                    │   └─────────────────┬───────────────────┘  │
                    │                     │ trusts                │
                    │   ┌─────────────────▼───────────────────┐  │
                    │   │      EKS OIDC Identity Provider     │  │
                    │   │  (registered in IAM per cluster)    │  │
                    │   └─────────────────────────────────────┘  │
                    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                         │
                    ┌────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐
                    │              AWS STS                        │
                    │   AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity                 │
                    │   Returns: temporary credentials            │
                    │   (AccessKeyId, SecretKey, SessionToken)    │
                    └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Step-by-Step Mechanics

### Step 1: Register the EKS OIDC Provider in IAM

Every EKS cluster automatically exposes an OIDC endpoint:

```
https://oidc.eks.<region>.amazonaws.com/id/<CLUSTER_ID>
```

You register this URL as a **federated Identity Provider** in IAM. This is a one-time setup per cluster. It tells IAM: *"I trust JWT tokens signed by this EKS cluster's OIDC issuer."*

```bash
# Using eksctl (common approach)
eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \
  --cluster my-cluster \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --approve
```

**Why this matters:** Without this registration, IAM has no way to validate tokens coming from your cluster. The OIDC provider acts as the **trust anchor** between Kubernetes and AWS IAM.

---

### Step 2: Create an IAM Role with a Scoped Trust Policy

The IAM Role needs a trust policy that:
1. Accepts federated identity from your specific OIDC provider
2. Restricts trust to a **specific Kubernetes namespace and service account name**

```json
{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    {
      "Effect": "Allow",
      "Principal": {
        "Federated": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:oidc-provider/oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/EXAMPLED539D4633E53DE1B71EXAMPLE"
      },
      "Action": "sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity",
      "Condition": {
        "StringEquals": {
          "oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/EXAMPLED539D4633E53DE1B71EXAMPLE:sub":
            "system:serviceaccount:production:payments-service",
          "oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/EXAMPLED539D4633E53DE1B71EXAMPLE:aud":
            "sts.amazonaws.com"
        }
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

**Critical security detail — the `Condition` block:**

| Condition Key | Value | Purpose |
|---------------|-------|---------|
| `:sub` | `system:serviceaccount:NAMESPACE:SA_NAME` | Restricts to one specific service account in one namespace |
| `:aud` | `sts.amazonaws.com` | Ensures token is intended for STS, not another service |

Without the `:sub` condition, **any pod in any namespace** on that cluster could assume the role — a significant privilege escalation risk.

---

### Step 3: Annotate the Kubernetes Service Account

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: payments-service
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/PaymentsServiceRole
    # Optional: customize token expiry (default 86400s / 24h)
    eks.amazonaws.com/token-expiration: "3600"
```

This annotation is the **link** between the Kubernetes identity system and AWS IAM. The service account itself has no AWS permissions — it's just a pointer to the IAM Role.

---

### Step 4: Reference the Service Account in Your Pod/Deployment

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments-service
  namespace: production
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: payments-service   # ← references the annotated KSA
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: my-payments-app:latest
```

---

### Step 5: The Pod Identity Webhook Does the Heavy Lifting

When the pod is scheduled, the **EKS Pod Identity Mutating Admission Webhook** (running in the cluster) automatically intercepts the pod creation and injects:

**Environment Variables:**
```
AWS_ROLE_ARN=arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/PaymentsServiceRole
AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE=/var/run/secrets/eks.amazonaws.com/serviceaccount/token
```

**Projected Volume (mounted JWT token):**
```yaml
# Automatically injected by webhook — you don't write this
volumes:
  - name: aws-iam-token
    projected:
      sources:
        - serviceAccountToken:
            audience: sts.amazonaws.com
            expirationSeconds: 86400
            path: token
```

The JWT token in this file contains claims like:
```json
{
  "iss": "https://oidc.eks.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/id/CLUSTER_ID",
  "sub": "system:serviceaccount:production:payments-service",
  "aud": ["sts.amazonaws.com"],
  "exp": 1234567890
}
```

---

### Step 6: AWS SDK Automatically Exchanges Token for Credentials

Modern AWS SDKs (v2+) include a **Web Identity Token credential provider** that:

1. Detects `AWS_WEB_IDENTITY_TOKEN_FILE` and `AWS_ROLE_ARN` environment variables
2. Reads the JWT from the mounted file path
3. Calls `sts:AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` with the JWT
4. Receives temporary credentials (valid for the token's expiration period)
5. **Automatically refreshes** credentials before they expire

```
Pod SDK                    AWS STS                    IAM / OIDC Provider
    │                          │                              │
    │── AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity ──▶│                        │
    │   (JWT token, Role ARN)        │                        │
    │                          │── Validate JWT signature ──▶│
    │                          │◀── Confirmed valid ─────────│
    │                          │── Check trust policy ───────│
    │                          │   (sub matches condition?)  │
    │◀── Temporary Credentials ─│                            │
    │   (AccessKeyId,           │                            │
    │    SecretAccessKey,       │                            │
    │    SessionToken,          │                            │
    │    Expiration)            │                            │
```

**This entire exchange is invisible to application code.** The app just calls `s3.GetObject()` and the SDK handles credential acquisition transparently.

---

## Security Properties and Isolation Guarantees

### Namespace Isolation
```
Namespace: production
  └── ServiceAccount: payments-service  → Role: PaymentsServiceRole (S3 read)
  └── ServiceAccount: auth-service      → Role: AuthServiceRole (DynamoDB read)

Namespace: staging
  └── ServiceAccount: payments-service  → Role: StagingPaymentsRole (different permissions)
```

Even if two service accounts share the same name, they're in different namespaces, so the `:sub` condition (`system:serviceaccount:NAMESPACE:NAME`) ensures they map to different IAM roles.

### Cluster Isolation
Each EKS cluster has a **unique OIDC issuer URL** containing the cluster ID. A compromised pod from Cluster A cannot assume roles configured for Cluster B, because:
- The OIDC provider ARN in the trust policy is cluster-specific
- JWT tokens are signed by the cluster's OIDC key, which differs per cluster

### No Long-Lived Credentials
| Approach | Credential Lifetime | Rotation |
|----------|--------------------|---------:|
| Hardcoded access keys | Permanent until manually rotated | Manual |
| EC2 Instance Profile | ~6 hours | Automatic |
| **IRSA** | **Configurable (1h–24h default)** | **Automatic** |

---

## Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

### Pitfall 1: Trust Policy Too Broad
```json
// ❌ DANGEROUS: Any service account on the cluster can assume this role
"Condition": {
  "StringLike": {
    "oidc....:sub": "system:serviceaccount:*:*"
  }
}

// ✅ CORRECT: Scoped to specific namespace and service account
"Condition": {
  "StringEquals": {
    "oidc....:sub": "system:serviceaccount:production:payments-service"
  }
}
```

### Pitfall 2: Missing `:aud` Condition
Without the audience check, a JWT issued for one purpose could potentially be replayed for STS. Always include:
```json
"oidc....:aud": "sts.amazonaws.com"
```

### Pitfall 3: Node Role Permissions Leaking to Pods
The EC2 node's instance profile (used by the kubelet) should have **minimal permissions**. Pods should rely exclusively on IRSA, not inherit node-level permissions. Use IMDSv2 with hop limit of 1 to prevent pods from accessing the instance metadata service:

```bash
# When creating node groups, set hop limit to 1
--metadata-options HttpPutResponseHopLimit=1,HttpTokens=required
```

---

## Relationship to IAM Concepts from the Knowledge Graph

While the graph lacks EKS-specific nodes, the IAM concepts it does contain map directly:

| Graph Concept | Role in IRSA |
|---------------|-------------|
| `aws.iam.roles` | The IAM Role pods assume via IRSA |
| `aws.iam.roles.trust_policy` | Contains the OIDC federated trust with namespace/SA conditions |
| `aws.iam.policy` | Permission policies attached to the role defining what the pod can do |
| `aws.iam.roles.constraints` | Session duration limits on the temporary credentials |
| `aws.sts` | Issues the temporary credentials via `AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity` |

---

## Complete Working Example

```bash
# 1. Associate OIDC provider with cluster
eksctl utils associate-iam-oidc-provider \
  --cluster production-cluster \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --approve

# 2. Create IAM role with trust policy (eksctl shortcut)
eksctl create iamserviceaccount \
  --cluster production-cluster \
  --namespace production \
  --name payments-service \
  --attach-policy-arn arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess \
  --approve \
  --region us-east-1
# This creates both the IAM role AND the annotated Kubernetes service account

# 3. Reference in deployment
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: payments-app
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payments
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: payments
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: payments-service
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: payments-app:1.0
          # No AWS credentials needed — IRSA handles it
EOF

# 4. Verify credentials inside pod
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n production -- \
  aws sts get-caller-identity
# Returns the IAM Role ARN, confirming IRSA is working
```

---

## Summary

IRSA works through a **chain of trust**:

```
Kubernetes issues JWT  →  EKS OIDC Provider validates it  →  IAM Role trust policy accepts it
     (per pod)              (registered in IAM)                  (scoped to namespace+SA)
                                                                          ↓
Application gets temporary AWS credentials  ←  STS issues them  ←  Trust verified
```

The elegance of this system is that **no secrets are ever stored or distributed** — the pod's identity is cryptographically proven by the JWT token, which is automatically mounted and rotated by Kubernetes itself.

This is a key distinction — provisioned concurrency solves the **cold start problem** by keeping execution environments warm and ready before invocations arrive.

**Pricing relationship:** Provisioned Concurrency has a `DEPENDS_ON` relationship with **Lambda Duration**, which is billed at **$0.0000033334 per GB-second** — the same rate applies for both on-demand and provisioned concurrency duration charges.
